book_summary: 2 Samuel chapters 1-8 are complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The batch covers the conflicting Amalekite messenger account and execution, David's lament for Saul and Jonathan, Judah's anointing, Ish-bosheth's rival monarchy, the Gibeon killing contest, Asahel's death, Rizpah and Abner's rupture, Michal's forced transfer and Paltiel's grief, Joab's murder of Abner, Ish-bosheth's assassination and the mutilation of his killers, David's national coronation, conquest of Jebusite Jerusalem, ableist blind/lame language, palace and dynasty expansion, Philistine wars, Uzzah's death during ark transport, Obed-edom, David's dance and conflict with Michal, the Davidic house promise, and imperial expansion through Moab, Zobah, Damascus, Edom, tribute, garrisons, plunder, and administrative consolidation. Safeguards reject summary execution, suicide and assisted-death misuse, xenophobia, queer erasure, war glorification, political marriage ownership, disability and disease stigma, corpse mutilation, sacred-object terror, infertility punishment readings, hereditary leader immunity, supersessionism, territorial entitlement, corporal-punishment theology, animal harm, occupation, tribute, and holy-war claims.

chapters.notes: Conflicting Saul-death account, Amalekite messenger, summary execution, Bow lament, Saul and Jonathan, and covenantal love.
Hebron, Judah's anointing, Ish-bosheth, divided kingdom, Gibeon contest, Asahel, pursuit, casualties, and burial.
Civil war, royal sons, Rizpah, Abner's defection, Michal/Paltiel, Joab's murder, curse, mourning, and command responsibility.
Mephibosheth's injury, Ish-bosheth's domestic assassination, beheading, providence claim, execution, mutilation, display, and burial.
National coronation, Jebusite Jerusalem, blind/lame rhetoric, City of David, palace labor, wives and concubines, and Philistine battles.
Ark procession, Uzzah's death, David's fear, Obed-edom, sacrifice, dance, food, Michal's protest, servant women, and childlessness.
Cedar and curtains, Nathan's correction, mobile presence, Davidic house promise, royal sonship, forever language, and prayer.
Philistine subjugation, Moabite executions, Zobah and Damascus wars, horse injury, garrisons, tribute, plunder, Edom, and royal administration.

translation_notes: The Amalekite messenger's account conflicts with 1 Samuel 31. His resident-alien and ethnic identity never explains guilt or supports inherited enmity.
David's immediate executions in chapters 1 and 4 lack recorded due process and may not become models of righteous state violence.
Jonathan's love for David may not be erased by compulsory heterosexual interpretation, while no single modern identity label should be imposed.
The Gibeon contest is lethal commander-organized violence against young men, not play or honorable sport.
Rizpah and Michal are persons rather than royal access tokens. Michal's forced transfer and Paltiel's grief expose dynastic control of marriage.
Mephibosheth's disability is not punishment or ruined identity; his nurse acted amid urgent flight from war.
The blind/lame language in Jerusalem's capture is deeply ableist and textually difficult. It may never support exclusion or contempt toward disabled people.
Uzzah's death is sacred violence and cannot be converted into victim-blaming or a claim that accidents reveal impure hearts.
Michal's criticism and childlessness may not be linked as sin and punishment. Her perspective, dynastic trauma, bodily boundaries, and political confinement remain open questions.
The Davidic covenant belongs within Jewish scripture and may not support Christian supersessionism, hereditary leader immunity, theocracy, or modern territorial entitlement.
The Moabite line-measure killing is mass execution. Horse hamstringing is deliberate animal injury. Garrisons, tribute, and plunder are imperial extraction.
Claims of justice and righteousness must be tested by the treatment of conquered peoples, outsiders, workers, women, disabled people, animals, and political rivals.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: 2 Samuel 1 requires maximum audit of the Saul-death contradiction, Amalekite/resident-alien terminology, crown and bracelet, execution logic, qesheth/Bow title, Book of Jashar, Gilboa curse, Jonathan-David love vocabulary, and lament rhetoric.

2 Samuel 2 requires audit of Hebron inquiry, reign chronology, Ish-bosheth/Eshbaal naming, territorial claims, Gibeon contest wording, Asahel's death mechanics, casualty numbers, and burial geography.

2 Samuel 3 requires audit of royal birth list, Rizpah accusation, Abner's covenant language, Michal's bride-price and transfer, Paltiel naming, Joab's gate killing, David's curse vocabulary, and political innocence claims.
2 Samuel 4 requires audit of Beerothite migration, Mephibosheth's name and disability tradition, duplicated assassination wording, providence rhetoric, execution, body mutilation, and public display.

2 Samuel 5 requires maximum audit of tribal covenant, reign data, Jebusite capture, blind/lame verses and variants, water-shaft terminology, Hiram labor, wife/concubine expansion, son lists, sacred images, and Philistine campaign geography.

2 Samuel 6 requires maximum audit of ark title, cart practice, Uzzah's action and death term, Perez-uzzah, Obed-edom identity, sacrifice, ephod and exposure language, food terms, Michal's speech, servant women, and childlessness.

2 Samuel 7 requires audit of house wordplay, tribal/judge variant, shepherd and enemy language, father-son covenant, rod language, forever claims, verse 19's difficult Hebrew, election rhetoric, later Jewish and Christian reception, and supersessionism risk.

2 Samuel 8 requires maximum audit of Metheg-ammah, Moabite measurement and killing, Hadadezer troop figures, horse hamstringing, Damascus casualties, tribute and garrisons, Toi/Joram names, Aram/Edom variant in verse 13, administrative offices, and kohanim/chief-official terminology.
next_actions: Continue 2 Samuel with chapters 9-16: Mephibosheth and Ziba, Ammonite humiliation and war, David's use of Bathsheba and murder of Uriah, Nathan's confrontation, pregnancy and child death, Tamar's rape and silencing, Absalom's revenge and exile, the wise woman of Tekoa, coerced reconciliation, Absalom's political campaign and rebellion, David's flight, Ittai and ark politics, Ziba's accusation, Shimei's curses, and public sexual seizure of royal concubines—with maximum anti-rape, anti-victim-blaming, anti-reproductive-coercion, anti-war-crime, anti-child-death-theology, anti-ableism, anti-misogyny, anti-incest, anti-retaliation, anti-leader-cult, and survivor-centered safeguards.
